step.title: Step 35
step.actionDisplay: UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 17 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions (first 10): UP, UP, UP, UP, UP, UP, UP, UP, UP, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 150, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Blue squares move within black chambers. UP/DOWN move them vertically, LEFT/RIGHT move them horizontally (apart/together). Squares are currently stuck at upper positions. Need to try MOUSE or find the goal. I've used many actions already.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 149 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 5.0x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After the timer bars reached 64 pixels:
1. The black objects merged! Previously there were 2 black objects (id=4 with 925 pixels and id=6 with 400 pixels), now there's 1 black object (id=3 with 1325 pixels = 925 + 400).
2. The left blue square moved to rows 14-18, cols 14-18 (higher than before at rows 29-33).
3. The right blue square stayed at rows 39-43, cols 44-48.
4. The timer bars are now 64 pixels each (full width).
5. The timer bar objects changed: previously id=0 was B (black, 15 pixels) and id=1 was W (white, 49 pixels), now id=0 is W (white, 64 pixels). Similarly for the bottom timer bar.
So when the timer bars reached 64 pixels:
- The black objects merged (the left leg chamber merged with the head/body)
- The left blue square moved to a higher position (rows 14-18)
- The timer bars became full (64 pixels)
This is a significant change! The black objects merged, which means the figure is now a single connected object. The left blue square moved to a higher position, which is now inside the head/body region.
Looking at the ascii:
- Row 15-18: Left blue square (SSSSS) at cols 14-18, surrounded by yellow (YYYY) on the left and black (BBBBBB) on the right
- Row 19-23: Black (BBBBBBBBBBBBBB) at cols 10-23
- Row 24-28: Black (BBBBBBBBB) at cols 10-18, yellow (YYYYYYY) at cols 19-25
- Row 29-34: Black (BBBBB) at cols 14-18, yellow (YYYY) on both sides
So the left blue square is now at rows 14-18, which is ABOVE the head/body region (rows 19-23). The blue square is in the yellow background, not inside the black figure.
Wait, looking at the ascii more carefully:
- Row 15: YYYYSSSSSBBBBBB
- The blue square is at cols 14-18, and the black figure starts at col 19.
- So the blue square is to the LEFT of the black figure, not inside it.
This is interesting. The blue square moved to a position outside the black figure. Maybe this is the goal state?
But the level didn't complete. So maybe I need to do something else after the timer bars reach 64.
Let me think about what happened:
1. Timer bars grew to 64 pixels
2. Black objects merged (left leg chamber merged with head/body)
3. Left blue square moved to rows 14-18, cols 14-18 (outside the black figure)
4. Right blue square stayed at rows 39-43, cols 44-48 (inside the black figure)
Maybe the goal is to move the right blue square to a similar position outside the black figure? Or maybe the goal is to move both blue squares to specific positions?
Actually, I notice that the left blue square moved when the timer bars reached 64. Maybe the right blue square will also move when some condition is met. Or maybe I need to move the right blue square manually.
Let me try moving the right blue square UP to see if it can also move outside the black figure.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The timer bars reached 64, and the left blue square moved to rows 14-18
# The black objects merged (925 + 400 = 1325 pixels)
# The right blue square is still at rows 39-43
# Let me try moving the right blue square UP to see if it also moves outside the figure

# First, let me check if UP/DOWN still work
result = action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        print(f"Blue after UP: boundary={n['boundary']}")
print(f"Level completed: {result.get('level_completed')}, done: {result.get('done')}")

# Let me try UP multiple times
for i in range(10):
    result = action(['UP'])
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    blues = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'S']
    print(f"After UP {i+2}: blues at {[b['boundary'] for b in blues]}")
    if result.get('done') or result.get('level_completed'):
        print("LEVEL COMPLETED!")
        break
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
